commentary: Nine activities versus thirteen — yes, the volume dropped, but honestly after last week's absolutely unhinged schedule, a little pullback is not a crime, it's basic self-preservation. The big story this week is that Saturday half marathon: same 13.1 kilometres as last week's run club, but your heart rate climbed from 151 to 159, which suggests you pushed harder or your legs were carrying a bit more fatigue from three weight sessions — probably both, you gorgeous overachiever. The swim heart rates are creeping up across the board too, Monday's pool session hitting 149 compared to last week's 139, which either means you're working harder in the water or your body is still cooking from all that lifting. Give yourself credit for threading three weight sessions through the week without the PT hand-holding, and then notice that the only real rest you took was a gentle morning walk — which, for the record, counts, and your body clearly needed it.
metrics_commentary: Okay so 6050 metres in the pool and nearly 16 kilometres of run-walk in two sessions — that's a lot of body moving through a lot of space, and honestly it shows, because your HRV sitting at 60 with a seven-day average of 56 means your nervous system is actually recovering like someone who knows what he's doing. The VO2 Max reading coming back as zero is just your watch being dramatic and losing the plot, not you — we've seen this glitch before and your cardio is clearly not the problem here. What I will say is that two run sessions across a whole week is a little thin when you've got this much swim volume carrying you, so the legs deserve more of the spotlight. More pavement, more showing off — the thong's not going to walk itself.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, four shows up — full house, no gaps, nothing to apologise for. The bench press PR is the one I'm screaming about: 31.7 kg estimated one-rep max up from 28.5 kg is not a small jump, that's over three kilograms of new chest, and I need everyone in this gym to know about it. Weekly volume climbed from 22,062 kg to 23,660 kg, which means the body is handling more and asking for it, and the numbers across the board — 154 kg on the quad, nearly 108 on the trap bar deadlift — are the numbers of someone who is built, actually built. This is a very good week to be Matt.
